data.title: GE Aerospace Q3: Strength In Order Book Continues
data.description:
I assigned a “Buy” rating to GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) in August 2024, highlighting their investment in MRO facilities. GE Aerospace released its Q3 result with a strong growth in its order books with a 25% sequential increase in its commercial engine deliveries. The strong order book will gradually translate into actual revenue in FY25. I reiterate a “Buy” rating with a fair value of $230 per share.

Commercial Air Traffic Growth & LEAP

On September 19th, Airports Council International (ACI) World predicts that passenger traffic will grow by 10% to reach 9.5 billion in 2024, which is higher than the GE Aerospace’s initial expectation of high-single-digit growth.

I think an interest rate down cycle will likely benefit global air traffic over time, as consumers have more cash flow for global travel, which could benefit GE Aerospace’s Commercial Engines & Services segment.

At the same time, GE Aerospace has been gaining market share in their LEAP ((Leading Edge Aviation Propulsion)), a high-bypass turbofan engine. With more customers starting to adopt LEAP, GE Aerospace has experienced low-single-digit market share gains every quarter. The company anticipates LEAP will generate profits during the year, and breakeven in FY25. I anticipate LEAP will continue winning market share and become a major growth driver for the company. It is encouraging to see GE Aerospace planning for LEAP's performance to align with CFM56 performance on the A320s by the end of the year.

Notable, in Q3 FY24, GE Aerospace’s commercial engine deliveries increased by 25% sequentially, with a 23% growth in LEAP program, demonstrating a strong demand for commercial engines and leading edge propulsion systems.

Q3, Outlook and Valuation

GE Aerospace released their Q3 result on October 22nd before the market open, maintaining a strong growth in orders, as shown in the chart below.

Thanks to the strong order growth, GE Aerospace raised the full-year guidance for operating profits and free cash flow while maintaining adjusted revenue growth rate.

I think their investment in LEAP service facilities started to bear fruit during the quarter. Their internal LEAP shop visits increased by more than 20% year-over-year. GE Aerospace also opened a dedicated LEAP MRO shop to elevate their aftermarket services to the next level.

As the company has delivered results for three quarters, I don’t anticipate any big surprises for the full-year performance in FY24. Looking ahead, I am considering the followings:

  • Commercial Engines & Services: As discussed in my previous article, GE Aerospace plans to invest $1 billion over the next five years to expand and upgrade their MRO facilities around the world. These facilities' expansion could potentially accelerate GE Aerospace’s growth in shop visit for their MRO business. In addition, I continue to anticipate LEAP will drive their market share gains and revenue growth. Considering these factors, I raised the segment growth rate to 9.5% from FY25 onwards.
  • Defense & Propulsion Technologies: The segment growth will be driven by the GE Aerospace’s unique propulsion & additive technologies, as well as increased defense budgets in western countries. I project the segment will grow by 7%, aligned with historical trends.

As such, I forecast GE Aerospace will grow its revenue by 9% from FY25 onwards. I continue to model 30bps annual margin expansion driven by 10bps from higher ASP for new engines, 10bps from reduced SG&A and 10bps from business optimizations.

The WACC is updated to be 10% assuming: risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.09; cost of debt 5%; equity risk premium 7%; equity $206 billion; debt $21 billion; tax rate 21%.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future free cash flow, or FCF, the GE fair value is calculated to be $230 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

GE Aerospace is in the process of divesting some non-core assets. They divested Electric Insurance, GE Licensing business, as well as some life and health insurance business in the past. I anticipate GE Aerospace will continue to unload these non-core businesses, which will lead to some near-term costs and have some financial impacts on the revenue growth on a reported basis.

Conclusion

I think GE Aerospace will continue gaining shares in their LEAP engines, driven by their investment in MRO facilities. A lower interest rate environment could potentially stimulate growth in commercial air traffic, which will benefit GE Aerospace’s business. I reiterate a “Buy” rating with a fair value of $230 per share.

data.title: Affirm: Still A Prime BNPL Player After A Moderate Pullback
data.description:
AFRM Remains A Prime BNPL Player After A Moderate Pullback

We previously covered Affirm (NASDAQ:AFRM) in June 2024, discussing why we had reiterated our Buy rating, attributed to its renewed growth tailwinds as Apple (AAPL) exited the Buy Now Pay Later [BNPL] market while providing opportunities for other fintech lenders, such as AFRM and other credit/ debit card providers.

With AFRM still reporting robust double-digit growths and the consensus quietly upgrading their forward estimates, we believe that the stock remained a compelling Buy then.

AFRM YTD Stock Price

Since then, AFRM continued to trade sideways before the painful market rotation in July/ August 2024, with the Fed's outsized pivot by 50 basis point in the September 2024 FOMC meeting triggering the much-needed reversal in market sentiments and its stock price trajectory.

The same recovery has also been observed in numerous fintech stocks, thanks to the lower borrowing costs at between 4.75% to 5.00% by the time of writing, with the market already pricing in another 25 basis point rate cut in the upcoming November 2024 FOMC meeting.

This is a critical development for fintechs such as AFRM indeed, since the "lower interest rate environment (may potentially) lead to increased consumer demand and a corresponding boost in" its revenues, on top of the lower cost of funding.

If anything, the BNPL leader has been able to sustain double-digit growth rates in Gross Merchandise Value [GMV], Active Customer base, and Revenues, despite the supposedly elevated interest rate environment over the past few quarters.

This is on top of the positive adj operating margins of 16.4% over the LTM (+18.4 points sequentially), implying its ability to operate at scale despite the challenging macroeconomic environment.

Combined with the strategic partnership with numerous commerce/ Big-Tech companies, including Shopify (SHOP), Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), Lowe's (LOW), and Apple, AFRM's position as one of the market leaders in the BNPL space is undeniable indeed.

These developments may also be why the management felt confident to commit to its GAAP profitability guidance by the end of the FY2025 (August 2025), with it further underscoring why the stock has outperformed on a YTD basis.

While the lower interest rates may potentially trigger the moderation of its interest incomes and subsequently, top-lines, we believe that AFRM's prospects remain extremely bright.

This is attributed to the fintech's higher merchant/ card fees arising from higher transaction volumes potentially balancing the temporal rate normalization, as observed in the segment's robust growth by +31.7% YoY in FY2024 - with it already signaling the platform's growing adoption and sticky consumer/ merchant base.

The same has been observed in AFRM's growing funding capacity of $16.1B (+3.2% QoQ/ +37.6% YoY), compared to the overall loan portfolio at $11B (+5.7% QoQ/ +26.4% YoY) - underscoring the lending partners' growing optimism about the BNPL space along with the management's execution thus far.

Perhaps part of the tailwinds may be attributed to AFRM being able to report a healthy loan portfolio performance, based on the relatively low delinquency rates at 2.4% for 30 days past due, 1.5% for 60 days past due, and 0.6% for 90 days past due.

This is compared to its credit card lending peers at 3% as of August 2024 and 2.67% in August 2019, with it implying AFRM's decent execution, perhaps attributed to its waterfall partnership with Katapult (KPLT), a fintech focusing on the non-prime consumer base and a lease-to-own business model.

With this strategy allowing AFRM to generate rich fees from KPLT (through subprime and near-prime borrowers) while maintaining an internally prime/ healthy loan portfolio, we can understand why the market is increasingly optimistic about its relatively robust prospects, significantly aided by the $50B in BNPL TAM.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, with AFRM expected to report FQ1'25 earnings call on November 07, 2024, readers may want to compare the management's execution to the previously offered GMV guidance of $7.25B at the midpoint (in line QoQ/ +29.4% YoY), revenues of $655M (in line QoQ/ +31.7% YoY), and adj operating margin of 15% (-7.7 points QoQ/ +3 YoY).

Given the consecutive double beats over the past six quarters and the relatively prudent FQ1'25 guidance, we expect the management to exceed expectations while potentially offering a relatively robust FQ2'25 guidance, if the fintech executes as per historical trends.

AFRM Valuations

This is also why we believe that AFRM remains compelling at FWD Price/ Sales valuations of 4.58x, compared to its 3Y mean of 5.88x.

Even when comparing to its high-growth fintech peers, including Upstart (UPST) at 8.38x, SoFi (SOFI) at 4.39x, and Block (SQ) at 1.86x, we believe that AFRM remains attractive after a moderate pullback.

So, Is AFRM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

AFRM 3Y Stock Price

For now, AFRM has already charted an impressive near vertical recovery after the worst of the August 2024 bottom, with the stock currently sustaining a robust uptrend support while running away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

With the stock now retesting the $44s support levels, interested investors may want to pay attention to its near-term movement, since the raised consensus forward estimates well exceed the management's relatively prudent revenue guidance by $9.44M - with a miss potentially triggering a near-term correction.

This caution is not overly done as well, since the stock market appears to be increasingly greedy, with elevated McClellan Volume Summation Index at 1,724.59x compared to neutral levels of 1,000x, and the CBOE Volatility Index increasingly elevated at 18.79x compared to the start of the year at 13.20x.

While we remain optimistic about AFRM's long-term prospects, we believe that the recent rally has been overly fast and furious, warranting a cautious approach.

While AFRM remains a Buy, we urge interested readers to observe for lower entry points, preferably at its previous support levels of $37s for an improved margin of safety - with those levels also triggering a more attractive FWD Price/ Sales valuations of ~3.5x.

Patience may be more prudent for now.

data.title: Moody's: Mission-Critical Solutions With Data Analytics; Initiate With 'Buy'
data.description:
Moody's Corporation (NYSE:MCO) is a global-integrated risk assessment firm, offering credit rating and data analytics to their customers. The company has been strengthened their data and analytics business via past acquisitions. I favor the company’s long-term growth prospectus and high margin business profile. I am initiating with a “Buy” rating with a one-year target price of $535 per share.

Expanding Into Climate & Natural Disaster Risk Market

In August 2021, Moody’s announced it would acquire RMS, a leading global provider of climate and natural disaster risk modeling and analytics, for approximately $2.0 billion. Through the acquisition, Moody’s has been expanding their risk management business with over 400 risk models covering 120 countries. There are several reasons why I favor the acquisition of RMS:

  • The acquisition boosted Moody’s insurance data and analytics business, contributing an additional $500 million to the segment revenue. RMS’s risk management solutions have strengthened Moody’s ability to offer integrating global integrated risk management services.
  • As both companies serve corporate and insurance customers, I anticipate tremendous revenue synergies. Moody’s can leverage an existing sales force to cross-sell risk management solutions, data analytics and credit rating business.
  • Recently, we have been experiencing more frequent catastrophic events due to climate change. Therefore, insurance companies and reinsurers have been incorporating more risk models to calculate potential liabilities, which created more demands for RMS’s services.

Delivering Mission-critical Solutions with Data Analytics

As shown in the table below, Moody’s offers a broad range of services including credit ratings, research & insights, KYC, and insurance workflow solutions. I think the company’s key strength lies in their mission-critical solutions with data analytics offerings. These solutions are essential to their customers, influencing day-to-day decision-making. As a result, Moody’s has sustained a quite high retention rate over the years. Moody’s has achieved around 110% customer retention rate with around 94% from retained base, 7% from upgrades and price increases, as well as 9% from new product sales.

The high retention rate has enabled the company to grow their annual recurring revenue (ARR) organically by 10% in the past. It’s worth noting that recurring business accounts for 95% of Moody's Analytics revenue. The robust growth in ARR has contributed to stable revenue growth and margin expansion, delivering more than 36% in reported operating margin. Going forward, I anticipate Moody's will continue to grow their Analytics business via internal R&D and acquisitions.

For instance, in 2020, Moody’s announced to acquire RDC, a leading provider of anti-money laundering and know-your-customer data and due diligence services, for $700 million. With the acquisition, Moody’s expanded their decision solutions business, offering AML and KYC SaaS solutions to financial institutions.

Recent Result and Outlook

Moody’s released its Q3 results on October 22nd before the market open. It delivered 23% revenue growth with a strong rebound in their Investor Service segment, as detailed in the table below. It is impressive to see transactional revenue grew by 70%, driven by increasing activities from infrequent issuers across all lines of business.

Due to the strong performance, Moody’s raised its full-year guidance with high-teens percent growth in revenue.

As the company has reported their performance for three quarters, I don’t anticipate any significant earning variance for FY24. As such, I project the company will grow its revenue in the high-teens percentage range and its expense by 10%. For business growth from FY25 onwards, my assumptions are as follows:

  • Moody’s Analytics: The segment includes Decision Solutions, Research and Insights, as well as Data and Information units. As discussed previously, recurring business accounts for around 95% of total revenue; therefore, the business growth is highly stable. I anticipate Moody’s will continue to sustain a retention rate of over 110% in the future, driven by mission-critical data solutions, new offerings and price increase. It is evident that Moody’s Analytics is a primary growth driver for the Moody’s total revenue growth in the near future. Considering their 10% growth in ARR, I project the segment’s revenue will grow by 10%.
  • Moody’s Investors Service: Moody’s offers credit rating for enterprises and financial institutions. S&P Global (SPGI), Fitch and Moody’s are the major credit rating agencies globally. Modor Intelligence projects that the US credit agency market will grow at a CAGR of 5.9% from 2020 to 2029. In my opinion, the credit rating agency market is very stable with limited players. Enterprises, public sector, and financial institutions need credit rating before their debt issuance and refinancing. Historically, these agencies have grown their revenue through both debt issuance volume and price increases. I anticipate Moody’s will grow their Investors Service revenue by 6% in the future, aligned with the overall market growth.

As such, I calculate Moody’s will deliver 8% organic revenue growth in the near future. In addition, M&A has been a growth strategy for Moody’s Analytics business. I project the company will allocate 5% of revenue towards M&A, contributing an additional 1.1% growth to the topline.

As a credit rating and data analytics company, Moody’s has been enjoying an adjusted operating margin close to 50%. For the gross margin, I forecast 10bps annual improvement driven by new product offerings with higher ASP. I also project 10bps margin improvement from reductions in SG&A expenses. Given Moody’s retention rate of over 110%, the business renewals from existing customers Given Moody’s retention rate of over 110%, require less sales and marketing expenditure compared to acquiring new customers, which will drive SG&A operating leverage. Due to the past acquisitions, the depreciation and amortization costs will vary each year, which will impact the overall operating margin. I forecast the depreciation and amortization expenses as a percentage of revenue will increase from 5.1% in FY24 to 5.8% in FY27, then gradually decrease to 5.6% by FY33.

The cost of equity is calculated to be 13% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 1.39; equity risk premium 7%. With these assumptions, I calculate the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be $535 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

  • In terms of credit rating business, S&P Global is a formidable competitor to Moody’s, serving a broad range of customer base including governments, structured finance, financial institutions and corporate customers. To sustain business growth, Moody’s must continue investing in their credit rating technology and platforms. Moody’s shareholders should closely monitor S&P Global’s performance.
  • As discussed previously, Moody’s is expanding their business into risk management and climate change markets. The new market expansion will introduce new competitors for Moody’s. For instance, Verisk Analytics (VRSK) specializes in insurance risk management and data analytics. Some of Moody’s business units may face strong competitions from these specialized players, who possess years of expertise in the field.
  • The debt issuance market is volatile and dependent on several factors, including interest rates, economic conditions, and refinancing activities etc. Consequently, Moody’s credit rating business growth will be volatile in the future, and investors should not be surprised by fluctuations in earnings.

Verdict

Through the offerings of mission-critical solutions, data analytics and risk management, Moody’s has successfully grown their recurring revenue base while maintaining high retention rates. Their tuck-in acquisition strategy further enables the company to expand their data analytics business. I am initiating with a “Buy” rating with a one-year target price of $535 per share.

data.title: Infosys: Mixed Results And Murky Outlook
data.description:
My existing Hold rating for Infosys Limited's (NYSE:INFY) shares stays unchanged. I remain Neutral on Infosys, following an evaluation of the company's latest quarterly results and its financial outlook. INFY reported above-expectations top line and below-expectations bottom line for the recent quarter. In my opinion, there could continue to be a divergence in Infosys' revenue growth and profitability going forward.

The company's guidance and project mix were the focus of my earlier June 18, 2024 initiation piece.

INFY Saw Mixed Results With Both Revenue Beat And Earnings Miss

Infosys reported its financial results for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 (YE March 31, 2025) with an earnings announcement released last Thursday. The company's most recently quarterly financial performance was mixed, taking into account its above-expectations top line and below-expectations bottom line.

Revenue for INFY in Indian Rupee terms rose by +5.1% YoY and +4.3% QoQ to INR 409.9 billion in Q2 FY 2025. The company's latest second quarter top line was +0.4% above the analysts' consensus projection of INR 408.2 billion according to S&P Capital IQ data.

The company highlighted at its Q2 FY 2025 analyst call that its top line was "ahead of our expectations" and noted that "Europe had strong growth and is now approximately 30% of our revenue."

In other words, the outperformance of Infosys' European business operations had translated into a revenue beat for the company in the recent quarter. Specifically, INFY's revenue derived from Europe increased by +16.7% YoY in Q2 FY 2025 to account for 29.8% or almost 30% of the company's top line in the latest quarter. As a comparison, the company's revenue contribution for the European market as a proportion of its total revenue was relatively lower at 26.5% and 28.4% for Q2 FY 2024 and Q1 FY 2025, respectively. Notably, Infosys' European business had secured five new "large deals" for the recent quarter as per its earnings briefing disclosures.

On the flip side, INFY's Q2 FY 2025 normalized EPS in local currency terms was INR 15.68 which missed the sell side's consensus forecast of INR 16.37 by -4.2% as per S&P Capital IQ data.

Infosys' normalized EPS in Indian Rupee terms increased by +4.6% YoY for the most recent quarter. But the company's operating margin contracted by -12 basis points YoY to 21.10% in Q2 FY 2025 and fell short of the market's consensus by -16 basis points based on S&P Capital IQ data. At the company's Q2 FY 2025 analyst briefing, INFY explained that its operating profitability in the latest quarter was hurt by "amortization of intangible assets" relating to M&A and an increase in costs relating to "higher variable pay."

Higher expenses drove INFY's operating margin lower for Q2 FY 2025, and this led to an earnings miss for the company in the most recent quarter.

To sum things up, Q2 FY 2025 was a mixed quarter for Infosys, considering the company's earnings miss and revenue beat.

Infosys' Outlook Is Murky Considering Various Disclosures

I think that INFY's financial prospects are murky with the company potentially registering higher revenue and lower operating margins in the quarters ahead.

In its Q2 FY 2025 earnings announcement, Infosys disclosed that it had revised its constant-currency top line expansion guidance upwards from +3.00%-4.00% earlier to +3.75%-4.50% now. However, the company has left its existing operating profit margin outlook in the 20%-22% range unchanged. My opinion is that Infosys is likely to meet its revenue guidance, but there are downside risks associated with the company's operating profitability guidance.

INFY cited "continued momentum" especially the fact that its "smaller deal (yearly revenue of under $50 million) pipelines have increased double digits over the last quarter" as the main reason for the upwards revision in revenue growth guidance at its recent earnings briefing. In my view, Infosys' clients are more willing to commit to smaller deals that involve a lower financial consideration than bigger deals in an uncertain economic environment. This means that a slowdown in large deal closure might be compensated by an acceleration in small deal completion, and this has given the company the confidence to guide for a faster top line growth rate.

On the other hand, I take the view that INFY might struggle to achieve its 20%-22% operating margin guidance. As a reference, the company's actual recent fiscal year or FY 2024 operating margin was 20.7%.

A key thing to consider is the company's pending increase in salaries.

Infosys' employee expenses will most probably rise in Q4 FY 2025 which would be a drag on the company's operating margin. At the company's Q2 FY 2025 results briefing, INFY revealed that it has a planned "wage hike" (exact amount unspecified) that "the majority of employees should get it in January" 2025 or the early part of Q4 FY 2025.

Another key to watch is the unbilled revenue balance sheet item.

INFY's top line increased by +4.3% QoQ as mentioned in the previous section, but the company's unbilled revenue grew by a more significant +6.1% QoQ. A faster rate of unbilled revenue growth vis-a-vis top line expansion indicates that Infosys could be billing its clients at a slower pace. This might suggest that some of the company's projects might be taking a longer-than-expected period of time (translating into a slower billing rate), which increases the risk of higher-than-expected costs being incurred.

In summary, Infosys' future quarters could be a repeat of Q2 FY 2025, where there is a divergence in the company's top line and bottom line performances.

Conclusion

I retain a Hold rating for Infosys. INFY is trading on par with its key peer, and Infosys' murky outlook means that it doesn't deserve a substantial valuation premium over its peer. The market currently values Infosys and Tata Consultancy Services [TCS:IN] at 28.2 times and 27.9 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E, respectively, according to S&P Capital IQ data.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams Q3: 5% Price Increase In FY25, But Overvalued
data.description:
I downgraded The Sherwin-Williams Company (NYSE:SHW) to a “Sell” rating in my previous article, published in July 2024, due to concerns about the stock's hefty price. Since that article, the stock price continued to rise as the market has anticipated a housing market recovery in FY25. While I view Sherwin-Williams as a high-quality company with strong leadership, the stock remains overvalued; therefore, I reiterate a “Sell” rating with a fair value of $290 per share.

Growing Residential Repaint

Sherwin-Williams started to invest in their residential repaint business in FY23, offering dedicated products and services to contractors. The management anticipated a strong residential repaint market despite a soft consumer sentiment caused by high-interest rates. A robust residential repaint business is critical to Sherwin-Williams’s business stability and organic growth.

As shown in the chart below, the new housing starts have been weak since 2022, as the cost of capital has made it difficult for developers and individual families. While the Fed began to cut rates, it is unclear when the housing market will normalize and return to their growth trajectory. As such, a strong repaint business will offset the potential weakness in the new residential markets.

In addition, Sherwin-Williams’s investment in residential repaint could potentially attract more PROs and individual contractors through dedicated products and services. Once the housing market starts to recover, Sherwin-Williams could leverage their existing base of PROs to grow their core coating business.

Q3 Outlook & DCF Update

Sherwin-Williams released its Q3 FY24 result on October 22nd before the opening of the market, reporting 2.2% same store sales growth in the Paint Stores Group, as shown in the chart below. Sherwin-Williams’s coating business demonstrated some moderate recovery with low-single-digit growth.

The company reaffirmed its full-year guidance, with diluted EPS of $10.30 — $10.60. My key takeaway from the quarter is the company’s momentum in the residential repaint market, which grew by mid-single-digit during the quarter. In addition, Sherwin-Williams announced a 5% price increase effective January 6, 2025, which could contribute to their FY25 performance.

For business growth from FY25 onwards, I am considering the following factors:

  • Sherwin-Williams is likely to deliver low-single-digit organic revenue growth in FY24, which will be much lower than their normalized growth due to the weak housing market. As the economy has entered an interest rate down cycle, Sherwin-Williams will likely start benefiting from a housing market recovery in FY25. Due to the low comparable in FY24, I anticipate Sherwin-Williams will grow its organic revenue by 10% in FY25, including a 5% price increase.
  • From FY26 onwards, I continue to project Sherwin-Williams’s business to normalize over time, delivering 6% organic revenue growth, driven by 2% pricing growth and 4% volume growth.
  • I continue to assume the company will allocate 2% of revenue towards M&A, resulting in 1% growth to the topline.
  • Over the past few quarters, Sherwin-Williams has improved their fixed cost structure for its manufacturing and distribution. In addition, the company has modestly increased the product price to offset some cost inflation. I continue to model 40bps annual margin expansion, driven by 20bps from gross profits and 20bps from manufacturing and distribution optimizations.
  • I updated the WACC to 9.5% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 5%; equity $97 billion; debt $10.5 billion; tax rate 20%.

With these assumptions, the company's discounted cash flow, or DCF, can be summarized as follows:

Discounting all the future free cash flow at 9.5% discount rate, the fair value is calculated to be $290 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

As I am assigning a “Sell” rating to Sherwin-Williams, I am considering the potential upside risks. Sherwin-Williams has spent $8.3 billion on shares repurchases over the past five years. These treasury shares repurchase could provide tremendous support for their stock price.

In FY24, Sherwin-Williams has benefited from a reduction in raw material prices for resins, solvents and TiO2. As China undergoes an economic downturn, local producers have increased exports of TiO2 putting downward pressure on its price. It is evident that a lower TiO2 price will benefit Sherwin-Williams’s margin in the near future.

Conclusion

The fundamentals of business recovery cannot justify their current stock price, as per my calculations. I am waiting for a future dip to own this high-quality coating franchise. I reiterate a “Sell” rating on Sherwin-Williams, with a fair value of $290 per share.

data.title: Equifax: Stay Buy Rated As Medium-Term Growth Outlook Remains Positive
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought about Equifax (NYSE:EFX) (published on 23rd July) was a buy rating because I expected growth to accelerate back to high single-digit percentages and that the business FCF profile will get better. EFX 3Q24 performance was solid, as mortgage-related revenue performed very well. As mortgage rates decline in the coming periods (with fed-cutting rates), EFX should be able to sustain this growth momentum. Near-term headwinds from non-mortgage-related revenue should ease themselves as the macro-conditions get better eventually.

3Q24 results update

Released last week, EFX reported its 3Q24 results, which included revenue of $1.442 billion. Reported revenue growth accelerated from 8.6% y/y in 2Q24 to 9.3% y/y in 3Q24. On a constant currency [CC] basis, total revenue grew 11% y/y. Revenue growth was driven by US Information Solutions [USIS] revenue growth of 12% y/y (split by 9.4% growth from Online Information Systems [OIS], 39.2% in Mortgage Solutions [MS], and 14.5% in Financial Marketing Services [FMS]); international revenue growth of 18% y/y CC; and Equifax Workforce Solutions [EWS] revenue growth of 7% y/y (split by 14% y/y in Verification Services and 19% y/y decline in Employer Services).

EBITDA margins contracted 40 bps y/y from 33.1% to 32.7%, and this was primarily due to a lower mix of high-margin EWS revenue and continued reinvestment related to the cloud transition. Despite the margin compression, because of topline acceleration, adj net income grew from $217.9 million in 3Q23 to $231.8 million in 3Q24, leading to an adj EPS growth of 5.3% to $1.85.

Growth inflecting upwards as expected

This quarter’s performance was a splendid one for EFX, as growth reflected just as I expected, and the underlying demand profile has become a lot better. Mortgage inquiries inflected positively to 1% y/y growth in 3Q24 from a 13% decline in 2Q24, and this marked the first quarter of growth since 2021. I believe this signals that we are in the very early innings of a mortgage volume recovery cycle, and this momentum should continue as the Feds continue to cut rates. Given the direct relationship between Fed rates and mortgage rates, lower rates should lead to lower mortgage rates, which is a positive driver for more mortgage volume. Remember that a bunch of existing mortgages are below the 6% mark, and the current mortgage rates sit just above 6%. I expect the next major surge in mortgage volume will happen when mortgage rates dip below this threshold, which seems likely to happen in FY25 (if the Fed cuts rates). If true, there is a good chance for mortgage volume growth to surge >10% if we use management-implied 4Q24 mortgage inquiry growth as a benchmark (they raised full-year mortgage inquiry guidance to a 7% decline from an 11% decline previously, which implies 4Q24 to see 9% y/y growth).

This surge in mortgage inquiries should drive a major acceleration in mortgage revenue growth, especially considering the price step up from FICO, which EFX has demonstrated the magnitude of in 3Q24, where the mortgage revenue within the USIS segment surged another 900 bps sequentially from 27% y/y growth in 2Q24 to 36% y/y in 3Q24.

I am also very encouraged by the fact that EFX continues to grow its TWN (The Work Number) database in Verifier and signed more exclusive partnerships (six new exclusive strategic partnerships, including Workday). The nature of EFX business is that data is king, and more data leads to more insights, which can be translated to more/better products for clients. In quantifying the size of new data adds from these partnerships, management sized the five partnerships excluding Workday at >5 million records (will get into TWN database over the coming quarters), and records from Workday were noted to be sizable. Given the scale of Workday (>$7 billion in revenue), I would imagine the underlying data and records available to be of huge magnitude. As of 3Q24, EFX had 182 million active records and 134 million unique records. The addition from these partnerships should easily push EFX to >140 million unique records over the next few quarters, expanding its coverage to >60% of total unique individuals.

Non-mortgage revenue is an area to watch

EFX’s non-mortgage-related revenue is still facing some challenges that investors should keep a watch on. In particular, EWS non-mortgage revenue growth decelerated from 12% in 2Q24 to 9% in 3Q24. It seems very likely that this weakness will persist as management cited weakening trends in EWS talent solutions, particularly with white-collar hiring slowing in September, softening EWS employer services revenue trends, and a push-out of some EWS government revenue from 4Q24 into FY25 (as the implementation of the work opportunity tax credit by the state is slower than expected). If growth persists at high single-digits or lower, it will undermine EFX's ability to achieve management's medium-term target for 13 to 15% revenue growth. Within USIS, the non-mortgage revenue also remained soft (up just 5%) as lending conditions remain challenging across all major verticals, including cards, personal loans, and auto loans, due to rising delinquency rates and macro uncertainty.

The way I see it, these weaknesses stem from the poor macro environment, where consumers spending is still soft (consumer confidence index dipped in October) and businesses are still wary on ramping up hiring. Once the macro environment gets better, all these headwinds should flip into tailwinds. The timing is hard to pinpoint, but historically, the US economy has always recovered, and I expect the same cycle to happen eventually.

Valuation

My model stays largely the same for EFX, except for my FY24 estimates, where I reduced growth expectation downwards to reflect management's updated guidance. While I am very positive on the mortgage side of the business, the non-mortgage-related business remained softer than I like it to be, and hence, I think it makes more sense to stay on the conservative side of things. That said, looking ahead, my view is that growth will see a surge in FY25 as it benefits from the step-up in mortgage revenue (driven by lower rates and higher FICO prices), followed by a step-down the EFX’s historical organic growth rate of 7%. My view for margin to expand stays the same, too. In fact, I am more confident that it will expand as EFX has already migrated 80% of its total revenue onto the cloud by the end of September, and the expectation is for it to reach 90% by this year. EFX has also started decommissioning legacy on-premise systems in 3Q, which serves as another catalyst for cost savings.

With a positive FY25/FY26 outlook, my view is that the valuation multiple can sustain at the current level of 30x forward earnings, the high end of the past 5-year trading range. At 30x today, the valuation is still not expensive when compared to peers (like Moody’s Corp., MSCI Inc., and S&P Global) that trade at >30x forward earnings with ~10% expected revenue growth.

Risk

Mortgage inquiries may not grow as fast as I expected if mortgage rates do not come down in tandem with the Fed cutting rates. While there is a direct relationship between Fed rates and mortgage rates, there may be a lag in the timing of mortgage rates coming down (note that mortgage rates went up in the last few weeks).

Conclusion

My view for EFX is a buy rating, as the 3Q24 performance reinforces my positive outlook. The accelerating growth in mortgage-related revenue, driven by improving market conditions, is a significant positive. While non-mortgage-related revenue faces near-term challenges, I believe these headwinds will ease as the broader macroeconomic environment improves. I am also very positive about EFX expanding its data database through strategic partnerships, as it helps position the business for long-term growth.

data.title: WK Kellogg: Minimal Risks And Strong Upside Ahead
data.description:
WK Kellogg Co (NYSE:KLG) is a mid-cap stock that has caught my attention due to its attractive valuation and positioning in the consumer staples sector. As I have written about before on Seeking Alpha, I am anticipating a value transition from growth to value as indices become increasingly overbought by exuberant bulls. This is not to say that I believe that a correction is headed any time soon (as nobody can predict this), but rather that I think liquidity is in the process of moving towards small and mid-cap companies as they offer better value for money per dollar invested. KLG to me is among the relatively small universe of stocks that I believe still offer a good deal to investors, and it presents a compelling opportunity as a newly independent company undergoing a significant transformation. With a forward P/E of 11.58x, a 3.63% dividend yield, and a clear path to margin expansion through its $500M supply chain modernization, KLG ticks many of my boxes and more, and I am pleased to enthusiastically provide KLG with a Buy recommendation.

Company Overview

KLG is perhaps among the most perennial and most visible consumer staples stocks that North Americans interact with in their everyday lives. Crucially, it spun off from Kellanova in October last year and now is an independent identity with a streamlined management team to unlock value from its operations. KLG is a staple of the North American breakfast, with its household names of Frosted Flakes, Special K, Froot Loops, and others featuring prominently in cupboards and grocers across the country. Despite its parent companies' 118-year history, its product line has remained robust, as the company boasts a 27.6% market share in the U.S. cereal category, while in Canada, its market share is significantly higher at 39%.

KLG has six primary manufacturing facilities throughout the U.S., which are then distributed to its customers, which include merchandisers, grocers, drug stores, and many others across the country. This delivery process is logistically intensive, and under Kellanova's wing, it has become a sprawling, (needlessly) complicated and outdated venture. To summarize a few key issues as outlined in its recent earnings call.

  • The Omaha facility has an aging infrastructure due to it being one of the oldest plants in its network. This is increasing KLG's operating costs via inefficient production processes.
  • Its Memphis facility is also reportedly facing challenges due to its excessive rice production.
  • On a broader level, its manufacturing hubs are scattered throughout the U.S. The logistical costs are high due to their geographic spread.

The good news for investors is that there are simple solutions to these issues, and simplicity is the solution that management has decided to go with. Its Omaha plant is due to be closed by 2026, while production at its Memphis plant will be scaled back by 2025. These optimizations are part of KLG's broader, $500 million supply chain modernization program aimed at expanding EBITDA margins from 9% to approximately 14% post 2026. Around $390 million of that $500 million will be spent on modernizing and installing new equipment at its facilities, with the remainder earmarked to streamline its logistics and production on a higher level. The intended net result is to have a smaller, simpler, and more reliable supply chain that costs less to operate and is easier to maintain.

What I like about this opportunity is the absence of excessive speculation on behalf of management as well as the minimization of undefined risk. The company's plan is concrete; a reader can follow along with the plan and logically deduce how cost savings could be reached.

To me, management revealing this plan shows that the company is already unlocking the expected value from its spin-off and becoming an independent entity, allowing management to respond faster due to the reduced overhead. There are some promising signs too in KLG's recent financials that suggest that an EBITDA expansion of 14% by 2026 is a realistic opportunity.

Financials

KLG has maintained their guidance for the rest of the year, but it will now be near the lower end. There is some cyclicality to its business, namely that typically during Q4 it's their lowest-volume quarter. However, on an adjusted basis, EBITDA is expected to grow between 3-5%.

Much of KLG's expected EBITDA growth I think can be chalked up to its strong gross margin expansion. The firm reported a 30% gross margin in Q2 of this year, driven by quick wins in operational improvements.

Something that, I think, is important here is that KLG is actually one of the best performances in recent years in terms of gross margin, so I believe that the firm is in a better position than what it appears. This is per comments taken from CFO Dave McKinstray:

Our underlying improvement is the direct result of our end-to-end focus and sustained improvements in our supply chain operations, including ongoing waste reductions. Gross margin for the second quarter was 30%, a 100-basis-point decline versus the prior year. Excluding the insurance proceeds, gross margin expanded over 100 basis points. Our underlying improvement is the result of our end-to-end focus and sustained improvements in our supply chain operations, including ongoing waste reductions.

However, there are some issues with KLG's financials, notably, that the firm reported significant volume declines last quarter at a 4.8% drop. It was highlighted by management that this drop was both unusual and somewhat alarming due to this drop occurring while its market share remained constant, which suggests that it's not a company-specific issue but due to a consumer pushback on price hikes for cereal products broadly. For the company, pain can be felt the most in its Special K brand, with its share reducing by 40 basis points year-to-date.

Other concerns for me is that KLG has guided for negative free cash flow this year, which will see its financial flexibility constrained over the next few years. It's still dealing with the burden of becoming an independent company (which cost around $60 million) and is working through its modernization spending of around $45 million. Thus, through its combination of it going through a transformative stage and net debt of $447 million, it expects its leverage to peak at 3x adjusted EBITDA in early 2026. Accounts receivable have grown to $313 million compared to $266 million in December, and its supplier finance program has also swelled to $158 million from $142 million in the same period. Due to these factors, I do not foresee any dividend growth for KLG in the near future, but what I do see is that its stock price will continue along its solid trajectory to head higher.

Valuation

KLG has had an impressive year-to-date, with its stock price climbing 31.28% at the time of writing. Much of that enthusiasm can, of course, be chalked up to it being a newly spun-off company and it reaping its early optimization benefits.

On a longer timescale, KLG's stock price has increased even more at 64.60% over the past year and 17.51% over the past five years. Despite this valuation increase, it still remains competitively priced compared to peers in its sector.

The company holds an A- rating on Seeking Alpha's quant rating for its valuation, with it being perceptibly undervalued based on EV/EBITDA and earnings metrics.

The consensus among analysts is also very positive, with its EPS expected to swell to 24% by December 2026 and to 43.29% to December 2027. If one were to take these two metrics into consideration, one could say that KLG is undervalued now and for the foreseeable future.

KLG might be trading at a discount due to its concerns as well as capital flowing into more attractive growth-based investments as the bull market rages on. However, I believe that KLG could be undervalued from a qualitative perspective as well. It fits into the sweet spot for investors looking for a pure-play staples stock that is also on a solid track to significantly improve its margins and profitability, with a reasonable and concrete plan to reach it. Installing new equipment and improving its logistics network are straightforward steps compared with the more ambitious (and far riskier) plans than many of its peers, and these steps also lead to tangible cost savings and greater efficiencies as well. It's a buy for investors looking for a company that is easy to understand and is going places.

Risks

Although I rate the overall execution risks of KLG's supply chain modernization plan on the low side, what I do see as a potential roadblock is capital constraints and lack of financial flexibility. As I noted earlier, it has a significant amount of net debt, and it is already tapping into its accounts receivables and credit accounts to navigate its transformation. According to the company's reports and earnings call, it has $644 in accessible capital, which includes $44 million in cash, a delayed term loan of $250 million, and a revolving credit facility of $350 million. When one combines its total transformation costs of $500 million, stand-up costs, and dividend payments over three years, it more than exceeds its total cash available, meaning that it will need to use these funds as well as its operating cash flow to fund its commitments.

Even a perfectly managed plan can go awry, and KLG's plans are significant. I believe then that there's a significant risk that either that the company will need to raise additional funds, or (as a more likely case) that the transformation will happen over a longer time frame than what has been stipulated. All available dry powder that the company has to work with has already been accounted for due to its plans. Investors must then keep the bigger picture in mind and accept the possibility that it may not unfold as effortlessly as management is predicting due to its liquidity position.

Takeaway

I like KLG due to the strength of its stock price, expanding margins, and its common-sense approach to unlocking thicker margins for investors. I anticipate that it may be a bumpy ride in seeing its supply chain modernization program through to its conclusion, but the overall execution risks seem minimal due to the plan's simplicity - although this does not imply it will be easy. The company's valuation is attractive, and if investors hold on tight, I believe that their KLG investment could be worth significantly more in the coming years.

data.title: YMAG: An Unconventional Method To Amplifying Your Portfolio Income
data.description:
Overview

I've covered some of the YieldMax funds in the past, and I am generally onboard with the inclusion of these high-yielding option ETFs. The problem with these 'ETFs' is that they still have an elevated risk profile since they are essentially linked to a single stock, rather than an index. YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund Of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) helps to alleviate some of these concerns by aiming to provide an underlying exposure to the Magnificent 7. For reference, the Mag 7 includes the following stocks:

  1. Apple (AAPL)
  2. Microsoft (MSFT)
  3. Amazon (AMZN)
  4. Alphabet (GOOG)
  5. Nvidia (NVDA)
  6. Tesla (TSLA)
  7. Meta Platforms (META)

YieldMax has created an option fund for each of these 7 stocks and the results have varied based on their respective levels of volatility, NAV health, and performance of the underlying asset. While there are some outliers in this group of holdings, we can see that YMAG has held up quite nice in terms of a total return comparison. On a YTD basis, YMAG has delivered a 22.5% total return as a result of the price downside and high rate of distributions to offset. Part of the appeal of YMAG is that it sports a massive dividend yield greater than 62%, according to the latest fund overview.

As a disclosure, I do plan to initiate a position in YMAG within a thirty-day span. Despite this, I aim to share an unbiased perspective on the benefits of this sort of option ETF, as well as the current risks and downsides that I see. I believe that these extremely high dividend yields are powerful tools to amplify the level of distribution income generated from your portfolio when used very strategically and intentionally. Creating a large stream of income from your portfolio serves as a buffer to your financial foundation that can help you navigate changing market environments and have cash readily available to take advantage of opportunities when they arrive.

However, these sorts of option ETFs are very unconventional in how they operate. Therefore, investors shouldn't take a traditional 'buy and forget' approach with these funds. Although this approach may certainly be viable during positive market cycles, I still believe that it requires a deeper layer of investor attention. With that in mind, let's first start by reviewing the fund's strategies.

Fund Strategy & Diversity

YMAG is still a new fund that has an inception dating back to only January 2024. Despite this short history, the price and NAV consistency has held up a lot better than its single stock counterparts. This is the main appeal for me because it has been able to preserve capital over time and maintains a diverse exposure to some of the strongest tech holdings in the world. According to the most recent prospectus, YMAG sports a very reasonable management fee of only 0.29%, with acquired fund fees and expenses bringing the total annual operating expenses up to 1.28%.

Looking at the top holdings, we can see that YieldMax's NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY) has the largest weight of 16.09%. This if followed by weight in YieldMax's AAPL Option Income Strategy ETF (APLY) and YieldMax's AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY), accounting for 14.82% and 14.29% respectively. However, it should be noted that the underlying positions are not actually held within these ETFs, and they instead maintain a synthetic position and make use of a synthetic option strategy. I pulled the following excerpt from page 3 of the prospectus.

As part of its synthetic covered call strategy, each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF writes (sells) call option contracts on its Underlying Security to generate income. Since the Underlying YieldMax™ ETF does not directly own Underlying Security, these written call options are sold short (i.e., selling a position it does not currently own). Each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF seeks to capture a portion of Underlying Security’s share price appreciation (generally no more than 15%) in a given month. To do so, the call options written (sold) by each Underlying YieldMax™ ETF will generally have an expiration of one month or less (the “Call Period”) and generally have a strike price that is approximately 0%-15% above the then-current Underlying Security share price.

This combination of its indirect exposure and use of a covered call option strategy effectively caps the upside price appreciation that can be experienced. The primary goal for YMAG is to capture a high rate of income by collecting the premiums from the options implemented, so there should be no expectation of price growth here. The idea is that the level of premiums that are collected are typically much during times of higher market volatility. Since the Mag 7 are growthier companies that can see large price swings, these stocks serve as a strong foundation for the synthetic option strategy. The fund's adviser, Tidal Investments, uses these elevated premium levels to fund the high rate of distributions.

The capped upside is just a side effect of using these options. To help visualize this, let's imagine that you write a call option on an asset priced at $300 with a strike price of $310. For this, you will collect a premium of $3. Now, let's imagine that the price of the underlying asset moves up to $315 per share. You would essentially collect the premium of $3 while also recognizing a price gain up to the strike price of $310 per share. That remaining $5 per share of price growth would essentially be sacrificed. Conversely, if the stock were to fall down to $295 per share, you'd see the full effect of the price loss, but this would be offset by the premium collected.

Dividend Utilization

What makes YMAG unique is that the distributions are issued out to shareholders on a weekly basis. While this is uncommon, it can serve as something that closely resembles a paycheck. This is one of the aspects that make YMAG such an attractive choice for those investors that may be looking for a way to supplement their main sources of income as a way to help fund their lifestyle expenses. However, these distributions are wildly varied and are issued at different pay rates week by week, as seen by the distribution schedule. So investors looking for a more consistent distribution should look elsewhere.

Rather than hold YMAG by itself, I believe that these distributions are best utilized when used in conjunction with a portfolio that already contains the underlying holdings of the Mag 7. These are companies with a history of innovation, rapid earnings growth, and have weak or nonexistent dividends. By using YMAG as an accompanying fund, you can effectively capture both growth and income. During bull markets, it's obviously better to hold exposure to the underlying assets that will allow you to gain from the underlying price increases. With the exception of Tesla, these companies have done exceptionally well over the last year.

Now, YMAG provides a way for us to still grow our portfolio during times where the market is flat or decreasing. Since these Mag 7 companies aren't known for their dividend yields, investors have to rely mostly on capital appreciation. Remember that saying: 'you can't have your cake and eat it too'? YMAG makes it so that you can do both. During years where the market isn't as strong and these stocks are trading sideways, YMAG is an excellent complement that allows us to now effectively profit in multiple market environments. Even during a bear market, while the price of these underlying assets may be decreasing, the income received from YMAG can offset the negative impact of any unrealized losses.

I was curious what the distribution makeup looks like for YMAG because there are some option funds that destroy their NAV due to the high rate of the distributions. However, the latest section 19a notice for September reveals that net investment income has covered 89.58% of the distributions for this fiscal year to date. Only 10.42% of the distribution has been funded by the use of return of capital.

Risk Profile

Speaking of return of capital, this form of distribution typically causes a decrease in NAV when used excessively. When the premiums collected from the options are not enough to cover the distributions, a fund may use return of capital to make up that difference. The downside is that this form of return of capital gets pulled directly from the NAV of the fund, which can contribute to a loss of value. These distributions can exacerbate a deteriorating NAV issue and add to the damage if the underlying asset is falling in share price.

A good example of this would be YieldMax's TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY). Tesla has struggled to keep up with the rest of the Mag 7 and experienced some volatile trading periods between 2023 and 2024. There was a point from the high of July 2023 where the price of TSLA fell by nearly 50% through April 2024. This poor price movement demolished TSLY's share price and ultimately made the high distribution yield of approximately 100% useless. Looking at the chart below, we can see that the price of TSLA ultimately recovered and now sits up by about 20%. However, TSLY never quite recovered because of the same underlying option strategy, which caps the upside price movements captured.

This is a real-world example of the NAV getting destroyed over time and the option strategy preventing a recovery. TSLY still issuing these high distributions alongside a falling share price only added more damage and this is a very real risk for the rest of the Mag 7 holdings. YMAG mitigates this a bit by providing a basic level of diversity. However, this diversity is essentially limited to the tech sector and there's a chance that the entire sector gets negatively impacted. This could be caused by a range of factors, like a recession, interest rate changes, higher inflation levels, or simply the results of the upcoming US election. YMAG can experience the same exact price and NAV decline in poor market conditions.

The tech sector has run up to massive premiums and may take a breather at any point. Imagine we enter a bear market in the future and the Mag 7 suffers from a year straight of declines. If all of the underlying stocks get negatively impacted, then YMAG would also experience a similar story. Since the fund is still so new and the market has remained strong, we have no idea of how this fund can navigate a down market.

This is exactly why I do not believe that YMAG is a simple buy and hold fund, no matter how bullish you are on the underlying equities. Another risk I want to address is that I see a growing trend of investors using margin out of their brokerage account to try and capitalize on the difference. For instance, I know that Fidelity has margin rates that sit around 13.075% for balances under $25k. This can vary based on your own portfolio size, but the idea here is that the huge yield of 62% would pay off the margin debt and the investor would pocket the difference. While this may sound like easy money in a green market environment, the damage could be life changing if an investor takes out too large of a margin balance and the market crashes downward.

While I am not totally against the idea, I want to put emphasis that this is a risky strategy that should be left to the investors familiar with managing this sort of risk, capital management, and have the time to actively manage their portfolio on a daily basis. The last thing you want is to be margin called during an intense bear market, and you don't have the necessary capital to get your account healthy. I plan to revisit this concept in the future and create a fully fledged out article on how to follow this sort of strategy responsibly.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that YMAG can be strategically used to boost the distributions generated from an investor's portfolio. However, I believe that a fund like YMAG should only be used if you have exposure to some of the underlying equities already. The Mag 7 stocks are growthier companies that aren't known for their amazing dividend yields. This means that investors have to rely on capital appreciation to provide the majority of the growth. However, YMAG gives us the opportunity to capture income during those weaker market periods where prices may not be growing as rapidly, enabling us to profit during different market environments.

While YMAG does eliminate some of the single stock risk that comes from other YieldMax funds, the diversity is basically limited to the tech sector. This does have a vulnerability by itself because this means that YMAG's NAV and price can deteriorate in a falling market environment. Since the fund is still new, we haven't seen how it handles this sort of market environment yet. Therefore, I rate YMAG as a buy but stay reserved with how much of a position I am taking for now.

data.title: ASGI: This 12.5% Yielder Should Be Considered By Retirement Investors
data.description:
abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (NYSE:ASGI) is a pure play infrastructure closed-end fund with a primary objective to deliver sizeable current income streams. Currently, it has over $600 million in AUM, and even though this is a relatively minor asset base from which to extract the benefit of economies of scale, the net expense ratio is acceptable at 1.65%.

It is also worth mentioning that ASGI is not that old of a fund, and has been publicly traded only since mid-2020. If we compare the total return performance of ASGI over this time period to other well-known infrastructure vehicles such as Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), we will notice quite strong correlations.

What is interesting about this chart is that, while ASGI has slightly underperformed BIP and UTF, it has exhibited much lower volatility levels.

With this context in mind, let me explain, why, in my opinion, ASGI offers an enticing investment case for income seeking investors, especially for those, who are near or already in retirement, to enhance their portfolio yields in a relatively conservative manner.

Thesis

There are four distinct aspects, which substantiate my investment thesis.

First, ASGI provides one of the highest distribution yields in the infrastructure vehicle space. Currently, the yield stands at 12.5%, which is significantly above that of BIP and UTF that offer, 4.5% and 7.1%, respectively. Just as BIP and UTF, ASGI has not cut the dividend in 2022 or 2023, when the sudden shift in the monetary policy caused many interest rate sensitive assets to massively decline. In addition, ASGI distributes the cash on a monthly basis, which in combination with truly attractive yield provides income-seeking investors a decent choice to enhance their portfolio's current income streams.

Second, the fund itself is strongly diversified not only at the individual security level, but also on the sector level. The largest exposure lies within the industrial and utility sectors, which are inherently underpinned by stable demand dynamics, especially when it comes to the underlying infrastructure assets.

Speaking of defense, we have to appreciate the beauty of infrastructure exposure, which comes with an embedded inflation-protection. In almost all cases, infrastructure investments are backed by either bankable long-term offtake agreements, which are signed before the final investment decision-making takes place or truly stable customer base, where the barriers to entry are very high (e.g., water utility service or telco companies in ASGI portfolio).

Third, as opposed to many high yielding CEFs, ASGI does not rely on external leverage to magnify its yield. This is a major advantage in terms of the risk management. It also has been one of the main reasons explaining ASGI's lower volatility levels that we saw in the chart above.

Yet, based on the annual report, ASGI has the optionality to source external leverage to a certain level:

Leverage. The Fund currently does not intend to borrow money or issue debt securities or preferred shares. The Fund is, however, permitted to borrow money or issue debt securities in an amount up to 331/3% of the value of the Fund's total assets, and issue preferred shares in an amount up to 50% of its total assets. Although it has no present intention to do so, the Fund reserves the right to borrow money from banks or other financial institutions, or issue debt securities or preferred shares, in the future if it believes that market conditions would be conducive to the successful implementation of a leveraging strategy through borrowing money or issuing debt securities or preferred shares.

In case management decides to assume debt, the chances are high that in such moment, the yield would go even higher. However, in my view, it is better to have no additional financial risk in the books given that the current yield is already attractive enough.

Fourth, since infrastructure is inherently a duration-loaded asset with a high sensitivity to interest rate changes, the fact that the future interest rate trend is clearly downward sloping (see FOMC dot plot chart below) bodes well for the income sustainability going forward.

In fact, falling interest rates should help not only from the income perspective by lowering interest costs for the underlying ASGI holdings, but also from the price appreciation angle, pushing higher infrastructure valuations.

Risks

Even though, in my view, the odds of experiencing a distribution cut down the road are very low, it is still worth factoring in two important areas of risk, which could potentially cause ASGI to revisit its distribution policy.

The first one relates to the fact that ASGI holds almost entirely pure equity exposures, which is not that characteristic to infrastructure CEFs. In many cases, we will observe an integration of some fixed income related instruments such as preferred securities or bonds, which would de-risk the exposure even further. In ASGI's situation, about 16% of the total asset base is placed in private equity securities, which tend to be even riskier than publicly traded common stocks.

While this is indeed a risk, having an equity component in the portfolio bring several benefits, such as higher income growth potential through increased dividends and the option for management to periodically monetize appreciated assets.

The second risk relates to the interest dynamics, where under a situation in which the Fed does not follow the indicated path of FOMC dot plot, or, in a worst-case scenario, decides to hike the base rates, ASGI's underlying holdings would become subject to elevated interest costs. This way, there would be a smaller margin of safety remaining in the books to weather unexpected financial shocks. The income growth potential would certainly suffer as well.

However, given the prevailing macro environment and the FOMC dot plot, experiencing a sudden change in the monetary policy or rising interest rates seems like an extremely unlikely scenario. On top of this, under such a scenario, the chances remain high that ASGI would stick to the current distribution policy since most infrastructure assets have their revenues linked to CPI escalators.

The bottom line

As outlined above, ASGI carries defensive fundamentals, which are underpinned by a diversified infrastructure asset base and no external leverage.

The benefit of an exposure towards infrastructure assets is not only bound by an attractive yield component, but it also introduces inflation-linked growth dynamics and relatively high upside from the normalization in the monetary base rates.

In my opinion, opening a minor position in abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund should be seriously considered by retirement income seeking investors, who want to enhance their portfolio yields, while accessing inflation-protected and interest rate-driven upside.

data.title: PSF: Much Improved, But No Inflation Protection
data.description:
The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:PSF) is a closed-end fund that income-focused investors can purchase as a method of achieving a very high level of current income from the assets in their portfolio. This fund is perhaps one of the more popular preferred stock funds currently in the market, which may be due to its respectable 7.26% current yield. This yield is, however, lower than many of the fund’s peers. We can see this quite clearly here:

As we can see, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund has a yield that manages to beat the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Securities Fund. In fact, it manages to beat all four of the well-known and respected preferred stock funds from that particular manager:
