The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund also manages to outperform the domestic preferred stock index in terms of yield. Over the past twelve months, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) has paid out distributions sufficient to give it a 6.07% yield. As such, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is likely to be more attractive than at least some of the popular options for those investors who are looking to add preferred securities to their portfolios. However, the fact that its yield is quite a bit lower than three of the other funds mentioned in the first table above does reduce its appeal somewhat for those investors who are looking to achieve the maximum possible level of income from their portfolios.

Another potential problem with the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund comes from the fact that the securities in which it invests offer very limited protection against the loss of purchasing power that accompanies inflation. As I explained in a previous article, the inflation problem that has ravaged the economy for the past three years has not been solved despite policymakers’ statements to the contrary. This is one of the reasons why gold prices continue to rise:

This chart shows the price of gold over the past year. As we can clearly see, it has been rising fairly rapidly and it is up about 35.6% over the past twelve months. Alan Greenspan, the former chairman of the Federal Reserve, has made a number of remarks that suggest that he believes that the Federal Reserve should raise interest rates when the price of gold is rising. For example, in an article in Business Insider from 2016, Greenspan noted:

The abandonment of the gold standard made it possible for the welfare statists to use the banking system as a means to an unlimited expansion of credit. In the absence of the gold standard, there is no way to protect savings from confiscation through inflation. There is no safe store of value. Deficit spending is simply a scheme for the hidden confiscation of wealth. Gold stands in the way of this insidious process. It stands as a protector of property rights. If one grasps this, one has no difficulty in understanding the statists’ antagonism toward the gold standard.

Forbes also points out that Greenspan tried to stabilize the U.S. dollar against gold:

Alan Greenspan stabilized the dollar still further against gold during the 1990s, the “Greenspan gold standard.”

The way that this was accomplished was by raising interest rates when gold prices went close to $400 per ounce and reducing them when gold prices trended towards $300 per ounce. He explained this during a mid-1990s speech.

In short, the fact that gold prices have been rising is a sign that the market believes that inflation is a bigger problem than the media and policymakers want everybody to believe. The market is usually a better barometer of these things than most politicians or government bureaucrats, so the fact that gold prices have been rising so substantially over the past year is a sure sign that investors may want to take steps to protect their portfolios against inflation. Unfortunately, investing in fixed-income securities such as preferred stocks and bonds as the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund does, runs counter to this goal. As such, any investors who wish to add this fund to their portfolio should ensure that they have sufficient exposure to other assets that offer better protection against a declining U.S. dollar (the root cause of inflation).

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund in mid-May 2024. The market for fixed-income securities has generally been strong since that time, as investors purchased preferred stocks and bonds in order to lock in respectable yields for their money before the anticipated easing of monetary policy. The Federal Reserve delivered on that easing by cutting the benchmark federal funds target range by 50 basis points at its September meeting. This rate cut allowed these securities to hold onto the gains that they produced during the buying spree that took place over the summer. As the assets that are held by the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund increased over the period, we might expect that the fund’s share price will have also delivered respectable gains.

This is indeed the case as shares of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund have risen by 7.31% since our last discussion:

As we can immediately see, the fund’s performance was better than that of either the domestic preferred stock or the domestic investment-grade bond (AGG) indices. The fund did, unfortunately, underperform the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period, but this is to be somewhat expected. After all, we do not ordinarily expect fixed-income securities to outperform theoretically riskier common equities. A common rule of finance is that securities with higher risk should offer higher potential returns. Overall, the share price performance is likely to be attractive enough for most income-focused investors, even though it has declined a bit since the start of October.

However, it is important to note that investors in the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund actually do a lot better than the share price performance alone would suggest. From the previous article:

The fund’s investors have received far more gains than are visible simply by looking at the fund’s share price. This is because closed-end funds such as the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund typically pay out all of their investment profits to their owners, which results in their realized returns being much higher than the share price performance would imply.

The fund’s distributions provide a very real return for investors that we should include in any performance analysis of this fund. When we do that, we get this alternative to the chart above:

This seems almost certain to please investors in the fund, as well as attract potential investors to it. As we can see, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund delivered a 10.72% total return since the date of our last discussion. This not only beat both of the domestic fixed-income indices, but it even outperformed the S&P 500 Index. Income-focused investors are not really used to outperforming the S&P 500 Index due mostly to the generally weak performance of income securities during the zero-interest-rate era that followed the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis. As everybody likes to beat the market, this is very nice to see.

Approximately five months have passed since our last discussion on the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund, so it is reasonable to assume that some things have changed. Perhaps most importantly, the fund released a new financial report that we will want to review. This report will, after all, provide a great deal of insight into how well the fund is covering its distribution. In addition to this, we have started to see the Federal Reserve reduce interest rates, which served as a tailwind over the past few months. This article will focus specifically on these changes and update our thesis as necessary.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the primary investment objective of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. The website states that the fund will attempt to achieve this objective by investing in preferred stocks and similar securities:

Regular readers likely know that I would prefer to have a more detailed description of this fund’s investment strategy than this. Fortunately, we can find a more detailed description of the fund’s strategy in its most recent annual report. This document offers the following description:

The Fund pursues its objective primarily by investing in issues of preferred and debt securities believed to be undervalued relative to credit quality and other investment characteristics. In making this determination, the investment manager evaluates the fundamental characteristics of an issuer, including an issuer’s creditworthiness, and also takes into account prevailing market factors. In analyzing credit quality, the investment manager considers not only fundamental analysis, but also an issuer’s corporate and capital structure and the placement of the preferred or debt securities within that structure. In evaluating relative value, the investment manager also takes into account call, conversion and other structural security features, in addition to such factors as the likely directions of credit ratings and relative value versus other income security classes. The Fund will not seek to achieve specific environmental, social or governance outcomes through its portfolio of investments, nor will it pursue an overall impact or sustainable investment strategy. However, the investment manager will incorporate consideration of relevant ESG factors into its investment decision-making. For example, although the investment manager does not generally exclude investments based on ESG factors alone, when considering an investment opportunity with material exposure to carbon emissions regulation, this risk may be considered as one factor in the investment manager’s holistic review process.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its Managed Assets in a portfolio of preferred and other income securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. companies, including traditional preferred securities; hybrid preferred securities that have investment and economic characteristics of both preferred stock and debt securities; floating-rate and fixed-to-floating rate preferred securities; fixed-and floating-rate corporate debt securities; convertible securities; contingent capital securities; and securities of other open-end, closed-end or exchange-traded funds that invest primarily in preferred and/or debt securities. These securities may be across a wide range of sectors and industries.

The description goes on to state that the fund will invest at least 25% of its assets in the financials sector, which we pretty much expect any preferred stock fund to do. The description also states that up to 50% of the fund’s portfolio might be invested in non-U.S. dollar-denominated securities, which I can appreciate. As I explained in a previous article, my expectation that the U.S. dollar will decline against both gold and certain foreign currencies going forward has prompted me to want foreign currency exposure in any fixed-income fund. After all, if this view is correct, then the assets held by such a fund may experience capital gains simply due to the currency in which they are denominated rising against the U.S. dollar. This would actually result in a rising income over time as these coupons are converted back to U.S. dollars.

Unfortunately, the fund’s website does not provide a breakdown of the currencies in which the fund’s assets are denominated. However, it does provide this country allocation:

We can see that the fund’s assets consist of securities from the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, the Eurozone, Switzerland, Australia, Japan, Sweden, and Bermuda. With the possible exception of the Bermuda-issued securities, it is a fair bet that many of the non-U.S. securities are issued in a foreign currency. After all, most of these countries are respected enough in the foreign exchange market and their currencies are trusted. There is no real reason, for example, for a Swiss bank to issue securities denominated in U.S. dollars when it will have no trouble selling ones denominated in Swiss francs. The same can be said of issuers in the Eurozone, the United Kingdom, Japan, Canada, and the other locations shown on the list.

The schedule of investments shown in the fund’s semi-annual report appears to confirm this. There are securities shown in the schedule that are specifically stated to be denominated in euros and British pounds. There are no other currencies specifically mentioned in the schedule of investments, so that could mean that the fund did not have anything except for U.S. dollar-denominated securities, British pound-denominated securities, and euro-denominated securities on June 30, 2024. That does not mean that these are the only three currencies that this fund will ever have exposure to, however.

In fact, the fund’s own documentation suggests that it will have Canadian dollar exposure at certain times. We can make this assumption from the fact that it was holding some Canadian dollar forward exchange contracts on June 30, 2024:

There would be absolutely no reason for the fund to enter into currency exchange contracts unless it owned securities that would be paying it in that currency (or is planning to buy securities denominated in those currencies). We can, therefore, assume that the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund may or may not be receiving income in Canadian dollars, euros, British pounds, and U.S. dollars at any given time. There may also be some other developed market currencies that show up from time to time. If the U.S. dollar declines against these currencies going forward, that could boost the income of this fund when measured in U.S. dollar terms. Ultimately, that is something that investors would probably like to see.

It is rather curious, though, that the fund has no money allocated to any other income-producing assets, such as bonds.

As I discussed in an article on the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund, some funds that advertise themselves as preferred stock funds have a substantial allocation to bonds. While bonds are usually considered to be safer than preferred stock, the inclusion of such securities is not always in the best interest of the fund’s shareholders. One reason for this is taxes, as preferred stock dividends can be eligible for income taxation at a lower rate than bond coupon payments. Thus, investors in preferred stock actually end up with more spendable income than investors in bonds, assuming identical coupon yields. As our lifestyles depend more on after-tax income than on pre-tax income, this results in preferred stock investors being able to live a more expensive lifestyle, all else being equal. It is nice to see that the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund did not follow the same path as its peers. This fund consists entirely of preferred stocks.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield of its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund is borrowing money and using that money to purchase preferred stock and bonds. As long as the purchased securities have a higher yield than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, this will usually be the case. It is important to keep in mind though that this strategy is much less effective today with rates at 6% than it was a few years ago when rates were near 0%. This is because the difference between the interest rate paid by the fund and the yield of the assets that it purchases is much less than it once was.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not employing too much leverage because that would expose us to an excessive amount of risk. I generally do not like to see a fund’s leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 33.78% of its overall portfolio. This is pretty much in line with the 33.60% leverage that the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which is quite surprising. After all, as we saw in the introduction, the fund’s share price has risen by 7.31% since the date that the previous article was published. That would ordinarily suggest that the fund’s portfolio increased in size, and if that is the case then its outstanding borrowings should now represent a smaller proportion of the overall portfolio than they did previously.

As has been the case with many fixed-income funds in recent months, the fund’s portfolio has not performed as well as its share price. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value from May 21, 2024 (the date that my previous article on this fund was published) through the present:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value is only up approximately 4.85% over the period. This clearly tells us that the share price has outperformed the underlying portfolio and that the fund is now more expensive than it was previously. We will discuss this later. For now, we can still see that the portfolio is larger than it used to be so the fund’s leverage should have declined more than it did. This fund might have increased its borrowing slightly to boost its gains when the underlying preferred stocks appreciated during the period.

The Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund still has a leverage ratio that is pretty close to the one-third of assets level that we ordinarily deem to be acceptable. It also compares fairly well to its peers, as we can see here:

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. To that end, the fund pays a regular monthly distribution of $0.1260 per share ($1.5120 per share annually). This gives the fund a 7.26% yield at the current share price.

Unfortunately, this fund has not been especially consistent with respect to its distributions over the years. We can see that here:

As I stated in my previous article:

Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, this fund was a fairly reliable source of income, but the recent cuts have undoubtedly reduced its appeal in the minds of those investors who are seeking a safe and consistent income from the assets in their portfolios. The generation of income is perhaps more important today than it was in past years due to the negative impact that today’s high levels of inflation have had on our standard of living. It costs much more to maintain a certain lifestyle today than it did a few years ago, so we need our incomes to be climbing with the passage of time. This fund’s distribution cuts have been doing the exact opposite.

The fund’s current distribution is the same as it was the last time that we discussed it, so there is no need to provide an update there. However, as we saw in the previous article, the fund failed to cover its distribution over the full-year 2023 period. Let us have a look at the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, to see if things have improved at all for it.

For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund received $9,610,538 in interest and $2,285,076 in net dividends (dividends minus foreign withholding taxes). This gives the fund a total investment income of $11,895,614 for the six-month period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $6,373,904 available for shareholders. That was not sufficient to cover the $9,093,309 that the fund paid out in distributions during the period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2024, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund reported net realized gains of $2,996,327 along with $7,904,180 in net unrealized gains. The fund’s net assets increased by $8,181,102 after accounting for all inflows and outflows during the period.

As we can see, the fund managed to fully cover its distributions solely through the use of its net investment income and net realized gains during the period. This is a good improvement from the previous year’s failure to cover, but of course, 2024 is not over yet. I do not see any reason to suspect that the fund will fail to cover its distributions over the remainder of this year as its net investment income will likely come in around the same level and it has a substantial amount of unrealized gains that it could realize to cover the rest. The only real problem would be if preferred stock prices were to decline significantly, but that seems unlikely in the near term.

Overall, investors in this fund probably can rest easy as it seems unlikely that it will need to cut its payout.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund has delivered a fairly impressive performance to its shareholders over the past five months. Indeed, this fund’s total return actually beat the S&P 500 Index over the period. It has also corrected some of the problems that it had the last time that we discussed it, such as coverage of the distribution. However, the fund does have the same problem as other fixed-income securities in that it does not have any inherent protection against inflation as an equity fund does.
